sections.heading: Training data
sections.text: Training data is used to fit model parameters and learned preprocessing such as scaling, imputation, and encoding.

sections.heading: Validation data
sections.text: Validation data supports model selection, hyperparameter tuning, threshold selection, and early stopping. Repeated decisions make it unsuitable as a final unbiased test.

sections.heading: Test data
sections.text: The test set is an untouched final evaluation. It must not be used to select features, thresholds, hyperparameters, models, or training epochs.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.text: Data leakage occurs when training uses information that would not be available at prediction time or improperly learns from evaluation examples.

sections.heading: Preprocessing leakage
sections.text: Fit scalers, imputers, encoders, and feature selectors on training data only. A pipeline applies the learned transformation consistently to validation, test, and serving inputs.

sections.heading: Temporal leakage
sections.text: Temporal leakage occurs when a feature uses future information relative to the prediction timestamp. Time-aware splits and point-in-time joins prevent this failure.

sections.heading: Precision
sections.text: Precision is true positives divided by all predicted positives. It answers: when the model predicts positive, how often is it correct?

sections.heading: Recall
sections.text: Recall is true positives divided by all actual positives. It answers: of the real positive cases, how many did the model find?

sections.heading: Thresholds
sections.text: A probability threshold converts scores into decisions. Select the threshold on validation data using the relative costs of false positives and false negatives.

sections.heading: Loss
sections.text: A loss function assigns a numerical penalty to prediction error. Training changes parameters to reduce this objective; a metric may instead describe decision quality.

sections.heading: Gradient descent
sections.text: Gradient descent updates parameters opposite the gradient of the loss. The learning rate controls the update size; an excessive rate can cause oscillation or divergence.

sections.heading: Feature scaling
sections.text: Features on very different scales create poorly conditioned loss surfaces. Standardization can make gradient-based optimization converge more directly.

sections.heading: Logits and cross entropy
sections.text: Classification networks output raw logits. Cross entropy applies a numerically stable log-softmax internally, so probabilities should not be passed through softmax first.

sections.heading: Training and evaluation modes
sections.text: Training mode enables behavior such as dropout updates. Evaluation mode disables training-only behavior, and no-grad prevents gradient recording during validation or inference.

sections.heading: Early stopping
sections.text: Early stopping selects the checkpoint with the best validation criterion after a declared patience. The final test set must not choose the stopping epoch.

sections.heading: Lexical retrieval
sections.text: TF-IDF represents documents with weighted terms. It is a strong, transparent baseline for exact terminology but does not inherently understand synonyms.

sections.heading: Dense retrieval
sections.text: Dense retrieval represents queries and passages in a lower-dimensional vector space and ranks by similarity. Its quality depends on the representation and evaluation domain.

sections.heading: Hybrid retrieval
sections.text: Hybrid retrieval combines lexical and dense rankings. Reciprocal rank fusion adds inverse rank contributions and avoids comparing incompatible raw score scales.

sections.heading: Chunk-size tradeoff
sections.text: Small chunks improve retrieval specificity but may omit necessary context. Large chunks preserve context but can dilute the relevant evidence with unrelated text.

sections.heading: Structure-aware chunks
sections.text: Structure-aware chunking preserves headings and section boundaries. Every chunk should retain document, section, source, and version metadata for evidence tracing.

sections.heading: Evaluate chunking
sections.text: Choose chunking with a labelled query set. Compare retrieval recall, ranking quality, context completeness, latency, and failure slices rather than relying on intuition.

sections.heading: Drift
sections.text: Data drift means the input distribution changed. Concept drift means the relationship between inputs and target changed. Drift alone does not prove performance degradation.

sections.heading: Delayed labels
sections.text: When production labels arrive late, monitor input quality, prediction distributions, calibrated confidence, and operational proxy signals while waiting for direct performance evidence.

sections.heading: Response
sections.text: An alert should trigger investigation with slices, lineage, and recent changes. Automatic retraining is unsafe unless validation, deployment, rollback, and feedback-loop controls are in place.
